meta.title: Mummies
meta.author: Lisa Ing
meta.focusQuestion: How did the ancient Egyptians preserve their dead, and what did they believe about the afterlife?

sections.title: Introduction
paragraphs: In modern countries around the world, including Egypt, people often are buried in coffins after they die. Sometimes the body is cremated, and the ashes are either kept by loved ones or are spread over an area that the person enjoyed, such as the ocean. But this is not the way it has always been. The <g data-word="ancient" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="Egyptians" data-rule="tells WHICH Egyptians">ancient</g> Egyptians turned their dead into mummies.

The <g data-word="ancient" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="Egyptians" data-rule="tells WHICH Egyptians">ancient</g> Egyptians are famous for making mummies. They mummified everyone from kings to pets by drying the bodies, rubbing them with spices, and wrapping them in strips of cloth. However, the Egyptians are not the only people that mummified their dead. There is evidence of mummification in many places around the world.

sections.title: What Are Mummies?
paragraphs: A mummy is a preserved corpse. Normally, a dead body decays very quickly. Bacteria in the air start decomposing body cells immediately after death. If the body is left alone, scavengers and pests, like vultures and flies, devour the corpse. In a mummy, the decay process is arrested, and the dead body is preserved for thousands of years.

The <g data-word="first" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="mummifications" data-rule="tells WHICH mummifications">first</g> mummifications occurred naturally, in areas that were too dry or cold for bacteria to grow. Bodies were preserved without human interference. Later on, people developed ways to preserve mummies artificially.

The first Egyptian mummies were buried in the <g data-word="hot" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="desert sand" data-rule="tells WHAT KIND of sand">hot</g> desert sand. It was so dry that the bodies dried out almost immediately, preserving them from decay. These dried mummies looked a lot like beef jerky. Their flesh became tough and hard.

Unfortunately, <g data-word="unprotected" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="bodies" data-rule="tells WHICH bodies">unprotected</g> bodies buried in the desert often were eaten by jackals, which love the taste of "human jerky." Egyptians tried to protect their buried ancestors by covering the burial site with rocks. Wealthier Egyptians buried family members in painted coffins. But the coffins, which kept out the jackals, also kept out the hot sand, and the bodies inside decayed.

sections.title: Mummification
paragraphs: Resourceful Egyptians developed ways to preserve the bodies before burial. When someone died, the body was given to a team of embalmers. First, the embalmers laid the body face up on a six-foot-wide embalming table. Their first job was to remove all the <g data-word="soft" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="organs" data-rule="tells WHAT KIND of organs">soft</g> organs that encouraged the growth of bacteria.

They carefully pulled the brain out through the nose with an embalming hook, making sure not to deform the person's face. The brain was not very important to the ancient Egyptians. They believed that the heart was the most important organ and that it was the center of thought and feeling.

The chief embalmer cut an incision in the left side of the stomach. From this hole, the embalming team pulled out all the internal organs except the heart and placed them to the side. The stomach, liver, lungs, and intestines were preserved individually in special jars called canopic jars. These organs accompanied the body during burial but were not as important as the heart. The heart was preserved inside the body.

Embalmers washed the body, inside and out, with palm wine. The alcohol in the wine killed bacteria. By this time, the body had probably begun to stink, a sign of decay. So the embalmers filled the body with bags of <g data-word="sweet-smelling" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="spices" data-rule="tells WHAT KIND of spices">sweet-smelling</g> spices mixed with natron, a special salt. Then they covered up the body and internal organs with more natron and left them alone for 40 days.

The whole mummification process took seventy days. Once the drying process had been completed, the embalmers removed the salt. The mummy became very light, since all the water—which made up over 65 percent of the body's weight—had been removed. The embalmers filled the inside of the corpse with linen stuffing, producing a body that appeared strong and healthy instead of shriveled.

Then they rubbed the corpse with more spices, mixed with wax and oil, to make it smell sweet. Next, a <g data-word="sticky" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="resin" data-rule="tells WHAT KIND of resin">sticky</g> resin made of tree sap was poured over the body. When the resin hardened, it formed a thin, protective shell around the body. This shell protected the mummy from bacteria and caused it to become darker in color.

Embalmers often applied makeup to the mummy's face to make it look more alive. They also placed jewelry on a rich person's corpse before wrapping it up. The resin-soaked wrap consisted of linen bandages the length of a football field. The wrap helped to preserve the shape of the body.

The best linen was saved for the outermost layer of bandages. Cheap linen was used on the inside, where it couldn't be seen. Magical charms and talismans meant to ensure the mummy's safety in the realm of the dead were put between the layers of bandages. These talismans were carved out of precious stones.

sections.title: The Afterlife
paragraphs: The ancient Egyptians believed that immortality depended on having a well-preserved body. They believed that a dead person split up at the moment of death into several parts: the ba, the ka, and the physical body. The ka was a person's ghostly identical twin. It lived inside the mummified heart and could not move from the tomb.
The ba, which represented the personality and spirit of a person, was a human-headed bird that could fly around freely by day. The ba and the ka joined to form the akh, a person's soul.

According to ancient Egyptian belief, a dead person's soul traveled through the <g data-word="dangerous" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="realm" data-rule="tells WHAT KIND of realm">dangerous</g> realm of the dead to a special palace of judgment, called Osiris's palace. Along the way, the soul dodged monstrous snakes and crocodiles while crossing huge rivers of fire.

Osiris, the god of the dead, judged everyone by weighing their hearts on a scale. If a person's heart was heavy with sin, it was thrown to the hungry monster beneath the scales. That person would die permanently. But if the heart was lighter than a feather, then the dead person would live forever—or be immortal—in the world of Osiris.

When the Egyptians first began to make mummies, only very rich or important people were mummified. They believed that only pharaohs and nobles deserved an afterlife. However, as the embalming process became easier and the Egyptians became richer, more people were mummified. The Egyptians eventually believed that everyone deserved to have a chance at immortality.

Since the Egyptians believed that the afterlife was exactly like life, they buried mummies with their favorite objects and tools. Wealthy nobles were buried with gold and gems. Children were buried with their favorite toys. Everyone was buried with clay representations of food and drink, and with clay figurines called shabtis—servants who would work for them in the afterlife.

sections.title: The Burial
paragraphs: Mummies were laid to rest in <g data-word="decorated" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="coffins" data-rule="tells WHAT KIND of coffins">decorated</g> coffins usually made of papier-mâché and beaten gold. Richer people were buried in a sarcophagus made of stone or a coffin of imported wood. Pharaohs were buried in up to four gold-covered coffins nested inside each other.

The coffins were painted with a representation of the person's face so that the ba could find the right tomb when it returned at night. Hieroglyphs, painted or chiseled onto the coffin, told the story of the person's life. These inscriptions also held spells to keep the dead person safe in the underworld.

Superstitions were common in Egypt. Most people believed that mummies would curse anyone who disturbed their tomb and stole from the dead. But tempted by the treasures buried with the mummies, thieves broke into even the best-guarded tombs. They often tore the mummies apart, looking for the gold and gems under the bandages. They were willing to risk death and punishment for riches. Most tombs were robbed within a few years of their mummies' burials.

sections.title: The Tomb of Tutankhamun
paragraphs: The Pharaoh Tutankhamun, also called the Boy King, died when he was only a teenager. We know very little about his life or his rule in Egypt, but he is the most <g data-word="famous" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="mummy" data-rule="tells WHAT KIND of mummy">famous</g> mummy in the world.

Tutankhamun's tomb was opened in 1923. Even though it had been robbed twice in ancient times, King Tut's tomb still held <g data-word="great" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="treasures" data-rule="tells WHAT KIND of treasures">great</g> treasures. The thieves had entered the second room, which was filled with Tut's gold chariots and statues. They stole about a sixth of the tomb's gold but were probably interrupted before they could finish. King Tut's mummy had not been touched.

King Tut was buried in a series of three coffins, one inside the other. The first two coffins were made of wood covered in bands of gold. But the innermost coffin was made of <g data-word="solid" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="gold" data-rule="tells WHAT KIND of gold">solid</g> gold and covered with gems. Tut's beautiful burial mask was also made of pure gold. Heavy jeweled bracelets encircled his wrists, and golden amulets and necklaces were draped around his neck.

sections.title: Animal Mummies
paragraphs: The ancient Egyptians mummified animals as well as people. Cats were especially <g data-word="sacred" data-type="adjective" data-modifies="cats" data-rule="tells WHAT KIND - holy to Egyptians">sacred</g> to the Egyptians. They represented Bast, the cat-headed goddess of happiness. Cats were kept as pets, and when one died, the whole family shaved their heads and eyebrows in mourning. Cat mummies were buried with their owners or at the temples of Bast.
Ancient Egyptians also mummified other animals sacred to the gods, such as crocodiles, ibises, and falcons. These animals were sent to the afterlife as offerings to the gods.

sections.title: Conclusion
paragraphs: Natural mummies have been found around the world. They can be found in the cold, acidic water of a marsh in Denmark, in the frozen depths of an Alpine glacier, or in the hot sands of a desert.

The dead have been preserved as mummies by many different cultures, from Buddhist monks in Japan to tribesmen in Papua New Guinea. The Incas in Peru, half a world away from the ancient Egyptians, mummified their ancestors in much the same way as the Egyptians.

While artificial and natural mummification vary in some ways, they are alike in one important way. All mummification results in the arrest of cell decay, which preserves the body for hundreds, if not thousands, of years.

We can learn much about ancient civilizations by studying mummies and the contents of their tombs. Scientists around the world study mummies in order to know more about how ancient people lived and died.

vocabulary.definition: the realm of life after death
vocabulary.sentence: The ancient Egyptians believed that everyone deserved to have a chance at immortality in the afterlife.

options: the period of time before a person is born
a type of tomb where pharaohs were buried
the process of preserving a body after death
the realm of life after death that the Egyptians believed in

vocabulary.definition: people who preserve corpses after death
vocabulary.sentence: When someone died, the body was given to a team of embalmers.

options: thieves who broke into tombs to steal gold and treasures
priests who read spells from the Book of the Dead
people who preserve bodies after death using special techniques
artists who painted hieroglyphs on coffin walls

vocabulary.definition: ancient Egyptian picture writing using symbols
vocabulary.sentence: Hieroglyphs, painted or chiseled onto the coffin, told the story of the person's life.

options: special jars that held the mummy's internal organs
a cardboard-like material used to make coffins
carved figurines that served as servants in the afterlife
symbols or pictures used as a form of writing in ancient Egypt

vocabulary.sentence: The ancient Egyptians believed that immortality depended on having a well-preserved body.

options: living forever, never dying
being buried with gold and precious gems
having a well-preserved body after death
traveling through the dangerous realm of the dead

vocabulary.definition: made into a mummy; preserved after death
vocabulary.sentence: They mummified everyone from kings to pets by drying the bodies and wrapping them in cloth.

options: is burned until only ashes remain
is buried in a sarcophagus made of stone
is preserved after death through drying and wrapping in cloth
is placed in a coffin with favorite objects and toys

vocabulary.definition: holy; belonging to a deity or connected to a religion
vocabulary.sentence: Cats were especially sacred to the Egyptians. They represented Bast, the cat-headed goddess of happiness.

options: very old and forgotten by most people
holy and connected to a religion or deity
dangerous and kept far away from temples
valuable enough to be buried with a pharaoh

vocabulary.definition: a person's soul, combining the personality (ba) and ghostly form (ka)
vocabulary.sentence: The ba and the ka joined to form the akh, a person's soul.

options: a person's soul, formed when the ba and ka join together
the human-headed bird that represented a person's personality
the ghostly twin that lived inside the mummified heart
the god of the dead who judged everyone's heart

vocabulary.definition: charms worn on the body for protection against evil
vocabulary.sentence: Heavy jeweled bracelets encircled his wrists, and golden amulets and necklaces were draped around his neck.

options: special jars used to hold internal organs
spells written on papyrus scrolls
golden coffins nested inside each other
charms worn on the body for protection

vocabulary.definition: special jars that held the mummy's preserved internal organs
vocabulary.sentence: The stomach, liver, lungs, and intestines were preserved individually in special jars called canopic jars.

options: hold the mummy's preserved internal organs during burial
store palm wine used to wash the body
contain the natron salt that dried the body
carry food and drink for the afterlife

vocabulary.definition: a special salt used to dry mummies
vocabulary.sentence: The embalmers filled the body with bags of sweet-smelling spices mixed with natron, a special salt.

options: a sticky resin made from tree sap
a special salt used to dry out the body
a sweet-smelling spice rubbed on the corpse
a type of linen used for the outermost bandage

vocabulary.definition: a sticky substance made from tree sap that forms a protective shell
vocabulary.sentence: A sticky resin made of tree sap was poured over the body. When the resin hardened, it formed a thin, protective shell.

quiz.prompt: The "resin" poured over the mummy was:
options: a sweet-smelling oil mixed with wax
a type of makeup applied to make the face look alive
a sticky substance from tree sap that hardened into a protective shell
a special salt that removed water from the body

vocabulary.definition: magical objects carved from precious stones, placed between bandage layers for protection
vocabulary.sentence: Magical charms and talismans meant to ensure the mummy's safety in the realm of the dead were put between the layers of bandages.

options: clay figurines shaped like servants for the afterlife
magical objects placed between bandages to protect the mummy
golden masks placed over the face of the pharaoh
scrolls containing spells from the Book of the Dead

vocabulary.sentence: In a mummy, the decay process is arrested, and the dead body is preserved for thousands of years.

options: arrested by the police
stopped or brought to an end
started again from the beginning
moved to a different location

vocabulary.definition: the human-headed bird made out of a person's personality after their death
vocabulary.sentence: The ba, which represented the personality and spirit of a person, was a human-headed bird that could fly around freely by day.

options: the ghostly twin that lives in the heart
a human-headed bird representing the personality
the soul formed when ba and ka join
the god who judges the dead

vocabulary.definition: a person's ghostly identical twin that lived inside the mummified heart
vocabulary.sentence: The ka was a person's ghostly identical twin. It lived inside the mummified heart and could not move from the tomb.

options: a human-headed bird that flies freely
the soul formed when ba and ka join
a ghostly identical twin living in the heart
the god of the underworld

vocabulary.definition: carved figurines that served as servants of the dead in the afterlife
vocabulary.sentence: Everyone was buried with clay representations of food and drink, and with clay figurines called shabtis—servants who would work for them in the afterlife.

options: special jars holding internal organs
clay figurines that served as servants in the afterlife
golden masks placed over the face
spells written on papyrus scrolls

vocabulary.definition: a deep cut made during a surgical or embalming procedure
vocabulary.sentence: The chief embalmer cut an incision in the left side of the stomach.

options: a type of jar used for organs
a deep cut made during a procedure
a sweet-smelling spice
a decorative pattern on a coffin

vocabulary.definition: strongly attracted or driven by desire for something forbidden
vocabulary.sentence: But tempted by the treasures buried with the mummies, thieves broke into even the best-guarded tombs.

options: be forced to do something against your will
be strongly attracted by something you want
be afraid of something dangerous
be bored by something uninteresting

questions.question: What is the main idea of this book?
questions.evidence: The ancient Egyptians are famous for making mummies. They mummified everyone from kings to pets by drying the bodies, rubbing them with spices, and wrapping them in strips of cloth.
choices: Ancient Egyptians believed in and practiced the art of mummification to preserve the dead.
Children in ancient Egypt were buried with their favorite toys and games.
The mummification process took seventy days from start to finish.
King Tutankhamun died when he was only a teenager and is the most famous mummy.
questions.explanation: The book covers how and why Egyptians mummified their dead — from the process to the beliefs about the afterlife. This is the main idea that connects all sections.

questions.question: What are embalmers?
questions.evidence: When someone died, the body was given to a team of embalmers. Their first job was to remove all the soft organs that encouraged the growth of bacteria.
choices: People who broke into tombs to steal gold and treasures from the dead.
People who preserved bodies after death using special techniques.
People who painted hieroglyphs and designs on coffin walls.
People who read spells from the Book of the Dead during burial.
questions.explanation: Embalmers were the specialists who preserved bodies — removing organs, drying with natron salt, and wrapping in linen bandages.

questions.question: How did the ancient Egyptians create the very first mummies?
questions.evidence: The first Egyptian mummies were buried in the hot desert sand. It was so dry that the bodies dried out almost immediately, preserving them from decay.
choices: They wrapped the bodies in strips of cloth soaked in tree resin.
They buried the bodies in hot desert sand, where the dryness preserved them naturally.
They placed the bodies in painted coffins to protect them from jackals.
They covered the bodies with natron salt for forty days.
questions.explanation: Before Egyptians developed the artificial mummification process, the first mummies were created NATURALLY — buried in hot desert sand that dried the bodies immediately.

questions.question: Which internal organ was NOT removed during mummification?
questions.evidence: The embalming team pulled out all the internal organs except the heart... The heart was preserved inside the body.
choices: The brain, which was pulled out through the nose.
The liver, which was placed in a canopic jar.
The stomach, which was cut out through an incision.
The heart, which the Egyptians believed was the center of thought and feeling.
questions.explanation: The heart was the ONLY organ left inside the body. Egyptians believed it was the most important organ — the center of thought and feeling.

questions.question: What happened to a mummy right before it was wrapped in linen bandages?
questions.evidence: Embalmers often applied makeup to the mummy's face to make it look more alive. They also placed jewelry on a rich person's corpse before wrapping it up.
choices: The body was covered with piles of natron salt for drying.
A sticky resin made of tree sap was poured over the body.
The corpse was filled with linen stuffing to look healthy.
Makeup was applied to the face and jewelry was placed on the corpse.
questions.explanation: RIGHT BEFORE wrapping: embalmers applied makeup to the face and placed jewelry on the body. This happened after the resin had already hardened.

questions.question: Which is NOT a detail from the section "The Afterlife"?
questions.evidence: Everyone was buried with clay representations of food and drink, and with clay figurines called shabtis—servants who would work for them in the afterlife.
choices: Mummies were buried with their favorite objects and tools.
Wealthy nobles were buried with gold and gems.
Only children were buried with clay figurines called shabtis.
After death, a person's soul traveled to Osiris's palace to be judged.
questions.explanation: The text says EVERYONE was buried with shabtis, not just children. This is the detail that is NOT correct.

questions.question: Why did King Tut's mummy become the most famous mummy in the world?
questions.evidence: Tutankhamun's tomb was opened in 1923. Even though it had been robbed twice in ancient times, King Tut's tomb still held great treasures... King Tut's mummy had not been touched.
choices: His tomb, opened in 1923, still held great treasures including a solid gold coffin and burial mask.
He was the oldest pharaoh ever discovered in ancient Egypt.
He was the first pharaoh to be mummified using the full seventy-day process.
His tomb was the largest pyramid ever built in Egypt.
questions.explanation: King Tut is famous because his tomb — opened in 1923 — still contained amazing treasures: gold coffins, a pure gold burial mask, jeweled bracelets, and amulets.

questions.question: Why did historians think robbers of King Tut's tomb were interrupted?
questions.evidence: The thieves had entered the second room, which was filled with Tut's gold chariots and statues. They stole about a sixth of the tomb's gold but were probably interrupted before they could finish.
choices: Some of the treasure was still there, suggesting they didn't finish stealing everything.
Bodies of the thieves were found inside the tomb near the entrance.
The tomb was left wide open with tools scattered on the floor.
A curse on the tomb killed the thieves before they could escape.
questions.explanation: The thieves only stole about 1/6 of the gold. The fact that most of the treasure was still there suggests they were interrupted before finishing.

questions.question: Which of the following mummification events happened FIRST?
questions.evidence: First, the embalmers laid the body face up on a six-foot-wide embalming table... Embalmers washed the body, inside and out, with palm wine.
choices: The embalmers applied makeup to the mummy's face.
The embalmers filled the corpse with linen stuffing.
The embalmers washed the body inside and out with palm wine.
A sticky resin was poured over the body.
questions.explanation: Washing the body with palm wine happened early in the process — after removing organs but BEFORE drying with natron, stuffing with linen, or pouring resin.

questions.question: What are hieroglyphs?
questions.evidence: Hieroglyphs, painted or chiseled onto the coffin, told the story of the person's life. These inscriptions also held spells to keep the dead person safe in the underworld.
choices: Special decorative jars with lids that represented minor gods.
Carved figurines that served as servants in the afterlife.
A cardboard-like material made from wet paper pulp.
Symbols or pictures used as a form of writing in ancient Egypt.
questions.explanation: Hieroglyphs were the ancient Egyptian writing system — symbols and pictures painted or carved on coffins and walls.

questions.question: According to the book, how does Osiris judge everyone who dies? Write a summary.
questions.explanation: Osiris judged everyone by weighing their hearts on a scale. A heart heavy with sin was thrown to a monster (permanent death). A heart lighter than a feather meant the person would live forever with Osiris.
opinionPrompts: How did Osiris weigh each person's heart?
What happened if the heart was heavy with sin?
What happened if the heart was lighter than a feather?

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: A mummy is a preserved corpse whose decay has been stopped, and mummification existed in many cultures around the world, not just Egypt.
keyDetails: A mummy is a preserved corpse where the decay process is arrested
Bacteria in the air start decomposing body cells immediately after death
The first mummifications occurred naturally in areas too dry or cold for bacteria
The ancient Egyptians are famous for making mummies but were not the only people to do so
distractors: King Tut died when he was only a teenager
Cats were sacred to the Egyptians

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: The ancient Egyptians developed a detailed 70-day process to preserve bodies, from removing organs to wrapping in linen bandages.
keyDetails: Embalmers removed internal organs through an incision, keeping the heart inside the body
The body was washed with palm wine, filled with spices, and covered with natron salt for 40 days
After drying, the body was stuffed with linen and covered with resin that hardened into a shell
Makeup and jewelry were added, then the body was wrapped in linen bandages the length of a football field
distractors: The ba and ka joined to form the akh
Thieves broke into tombs to steal gold

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: The ancient Egyptians believed the soul survived death and traveled to Osiris's palace, where the heart was weighed to determine if the person would live forever.
keyDetails: A dead person split into parts: the ba (personality bird), the ka (ghostly twin), and together they formed the akh (soul)
The soul traveled through dangers to Osiris's palace, where the heart was weighed against a feather
If the heart was lighter than a feather, the person would live forever; if heavy with sin, it was thrown to a monster
Everyone was buried with objects for the afterlife, including shabtis — clay servants who would work for them
distractors: The embalmers pulled the brain out through the nose
Hieroglyphs told the story of the person's life on the coffin

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: King Tutankhamun, the Boy King who died as a teenager, is the most famous mummy because his tomb contained incredible treasures including a solid gold coffin and burial mask.
keyDetails: Tutankhamun's tomb was opened in 1923, even though it had been robbed twice in ancient times
Thieves stole about a sixth of the gold but were interrupted before finishing
King Tut was buried in three nested coffins — the innermost made of solid gold
His beautiful burial mask was also made of pure gold, with jeweled bracelets and amulets
distractors: Cats were buried with their owners at the temples of Bast
The mummification process took seventy days

intro.title: Mummy
intro.content: A preserved corpse — a dead body that doesn't decay! The Egyptians turned their dead into mummies to preserve them for thousands of years.

intro.title: Adjective: Ancient
intro.content: 'Ancient' describes something very, very old. 'Ancient Egyptians' = the Egyptians from thousands of years ago!

whatAre.title: Decay / Decompose
whatAre.content: When a body decays or decomposes, it rots and falls apart. Bacteria in the air start this process immediately after death.

whatAre.title: Arrested
whatAre.content: Here it means STOPPED! The decay process is 'arrested' = stopped. The body doesn't rot anymore.

whatAre.title: Natural vs. Artificial
whatAre.content: First mummies = NATURAL (hot sand dried them). Later = ARTIFICIAL (embalmers used tools, salt, and bandages).

mummification.title: Natron
mummification.content: A special salt (similar to baking soda) that DRIES out the body. They covered the body in natron for 40 days!

mummification.title: Canopic Jars
mummification.content: Special jars with decorative lids for the stomach, liver, lungs, intestines. Each lid represented a protective god!

mummification.title: Adjective: Sweet-smelling
mummification.content: 'Sweet-smelling spices' — 'sweet-smelling' is an adjective describing WHAT KIND of spices. Can you find more adjectives?

mummification.title: 70-Day Process
mummification.content: Remove organs → Wash with palm wine → Fill with spices + natron (40 days) → Remove salt → Stuff with linen → Rub with spices + wax → Pour resin → Apply makeup → Wrap in bandages!

afterlife.title: Ba, Ka, Akh
afterlife.content: Ba = personality (human-headed bird that flies by day). Ka = ghostly twin (lives in the heart). Akh = soul (ba + ka joined together).

afterlife.title: Osiris's Judgment
afterlife.content: Osiris weighed the heart on a scale. Heart heavy with sin → thrown to monster (permanent death). Heart lighter than a feather → live forever!

afterlife.title: Shabtis
afterlife.content: Clay figurines buried with EVERYONE (not just children!) to serve as servants in the afterlife. Depending on rank, they could be clay, stone, wood, or gold.

burial.title: Hieroglyphs
burial.content: Ancient Egyptian picture writing! Painted or carved on coffins, they told the story of the person's life AND held magic spells.

burial.title: Tomb Robbers
burial.content: Most tombs were robbed within a FEW YEARS of burial! People were tempted by gold and gems, even though they believed in the mummy's curse.

tutankhamun.title: Amulets
tutankhamun.content: Golden charms for protection. King Tut had golden amulets and necklaces draped around his neck, plus a solid gold burial mask!

tutankhamun.title: King Tut's Treasures
tutankhamun.content: 3 nested coffins (2 wood+gold, 1 SOLID GOLD). Gold mask. Jeweled bracelets. Even though robbed twice, most treasure was still there!

animalMummies.title: Sacred
animalMummies.content: Holy! Cats were sacred to the Egyptians because they represented Bast, the cat-headed goddess. When a cat died, the whole family shaved their heads!

animalMummies.title: Animal Offerings
animalMummies.content: Egyptians also mummified crocodiles, ibises, and falcons — all sacred animals sent to the afterlife as offerings to the gods.

conclusion.title: Mummies Worldwide
conclusion.content: Natural mummies found in Denmark (bogs), Alps (glaciers), deserts! Incas in Peru did it too. ALL mummification stops cell decay.

grammar.title: Adjectives
grammar.explanation: Adjectives are describing words. They tell WHAT KIND, HOW MANY, or WHICH ONE about a noun.
rules: Adjectives describe nouns: the mysterious death, the beautiful mask
They tell what kind: monstrous snakes, huge rivers, pure gold
They tell how many: four sons, seventy days, three coffins
They tell which one: the innermost coffin, the first mummifications

exercises.sentence: Along the way, the soul dodged monstrous snakes and crocodiles while crossing huge rivers of fire.
exercises.hint: Which words describe the snakes and rivers?

exercises.sentence: Tut's beautiful burial mask was also made of pure gold.
exercises.hint: Which words describe the mask and the gold?

exercises.sentence: The ancient Egyptians are famous for making mummies.
Wealthier Egyptians buried family members in painted coffins.
But the innermost coffin was made of solid gold and covered with gems.
exercises.hint: Which words describe the coffin and the gold?

exercises.question: Which word is an adjective?
exercises.sentence: The dead have been preserved as mummies by many different cultures.
options: preserved
mummies
different
cultures
exercises.explanation: 'Different' is an adjective describing 'cultures' — it tells WHAT KIND of cultures.

exercises.question: Which word is an adjective?
exercises.sentence: Magical charms and talismans were put between the layers of bandages.
options: charms
Magical
layers
bandages
exercises.explanation: 'Magical' is an adjective describing 'charms' — it tells WHAT KIND of charms.

exercises.sentence: The _____ (danger/dangerous) journey took the soul through rivers of fire.
exercises.explanation: 'Dangerous' is the adjective form. It describes the journey.

exercises.sentence: The _____ (beauty/beautiful) mask covered the pharaoh's face.
exercises.explanation: 'Beautiful' is the adjective form. It describes the mask.

exercises.sentence: The _____ (power/powerful) god Osiris judged the dead.
exercises.explanation: 'Powerful' is the adjective form. It describes the god Osiris.
